per_task.text:
We want n such that n² ≡ -1 (mod 17). Since 4² = 16 ≡ -1 (mod 17), n ≡ ±4 (mod 17). The smallest positive n is 4.

"Answer: 4"

We need n^2 ≡ −1 (mod 17). Compute small squares mod 17: 4^2 = 16 ≡ −1 (mod 17), so n ≡ ±4 (mod 17), i.e. n = 4 or 13 (mod 17). The smallest positive such n is 4 (and indeed 4^2 + 1 = 17).

Answer: 4

The product over all 13th roots of unity (including 1) of \((2 - 2\omega^k + \omega^{2k})\) simplifies to \(\prod_{\zeta^{13}=1} ((\zeta-1)^2+1)\). Since the factor for \(\zeta=1\) is 1, the product equals \(\prod_{\zeta\neq1} ((\zeta-1)^2+1)\). Let \(y = \zeta-1\); then \(y\) runs over the nonzero roots of \((y+1)^{13}-1=0\), which satisfy \(S(y) = \frac{(1+y)^{13}-1}{y}=0\). The product becomes \(\prod_{y} (y^2+1) = S(i)S(-i)\) because the leading coefficient of \(S(y)\) is 1. Evaluating \(S(i) = \frac{(1+i)^{13}-1}{i}\) and \(S(-i) = \frac{(1-i)^{13}-1}{-i}\) gives \(P = ((1+i)^{13}-1)((1-i)^{13}-1) = |(1+i)^{13}-1|^2\). Compute \((1+i)^{13} = (1+i)^{12}(1+i) = ((1+i)^4)^3(1+i) = ((-4)^3)(1+i) = -64(1+i) = -64-64i\). Then \((1+i)^{13}-1 = -65-64i\), so \(P = (-65)^2+(-64)^2 = 4225+4096=
